items.prompt: A function must materialize k output records. What time lower bound follows immediately under a unit-cost write model?

options.text: Ω(k).
Ω(1) only.
Ω(log k).
No lower bound follows from output.

feedback.reason: Every materialized result record must be written or otherwise produced.
feedback.details: This lower bound is independent of how quickly the records are discovered.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.omega_1: Writing k distinct records requires at least k output actions.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.omega_log_k: A logarithmic number of writes cannot materialize k records.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.zero: The output contract itself forces work.

items.prompt: Why can an existence query be asymptotically easier than listing every witness?

options.text: The decision problem may stop after one proof, while enumeration must produce all k witnesses.
Existence queries never inspect input.
Listing k witnesses is always O(1).
Decision and enumeration always have identical lower bounds.

feedback.reason: Result contracts create different unavoidable work.
feedback.details: A decision answer is constant-size, whereas enumeration carries an Ω(k) output lower bound.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.existence_no_input: They may still require substantial search.

items.prompt: A function returns a new list containing k matches and uses only constant working state. Which space classification is precise?

options.text: O(k) output space and O(1) auxiliary space.
O(k) auxiliary space only.
The same list counts as both output and auxiliary storage.
All space is O(1).

feedback.reason: Required result storage is output space.
feedback.details: The internal working state can remain constant even when output is large.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.aux_k: The list is the required result.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.both_k: One structure should not be double-counted.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.all_constant: The returned list grows with k.

items.prompt: A task asks for every unordered pair among n distinct items. What unavoidable output count appears?

options.text: n(n - 1)/2, which is Θ(n²).
n pairs.
n! pairs.
One pair.

feedback.reason: The result size itself is quadratic.
feedback.details: An O(n²) materializing algorithm may therefore be output-optimal up to constants.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.linear_pairs: Each item pairs with many others.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.constant_pairs: The contract asks for every pair.

items.prompt: What is the total number of element occurrences across all materialized subsets of n indexed elements?

options.text: n·2^(n - 1), which is Θ(n·2^n).
Exactly 2^n element occurrences.
n².

feedback.reason: Each element appears in exactly half of the 2^n subsets.
feedback.details: Summing over n elements gives n·2^(n - 1) emitted occurrences.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.two_n_only: 2^n counts subsets, not total elements across them.

items.prompt: Can returning a symbolic generator handle avoid an Ω(k) materialization bound for a contract that explicitly requires all k records stored in memory?

options.text: No. A lazy handle changes the output contract unless the caller accepts streaming or deferred production.
Yes. A pointer always represents all records at zero cost under the same contract.
Every output can be compressed to O(1) without changing semantics.
Output requirements never affect complexity.

feedback.reason: Compression, laziness, and symbolic descriptions can be valid only when the interface permits them.
feedback.details: They should not be used to claim a smaller bound for a different materialized-output contract.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.yes_same: The records have not been materialized.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.yes_compress_any: Arbitrary records may not have such a representation.

items.prompt: A function streams k results one at a time and retains only constant local state. Which statement is correct?

options.text: It performs Ω(k) output work while using O(1) internal auxiliary space.
There is no output because no list is returned.
The function necessarily retains all k results as auxiliary memory.
One-at-a-time emission makes total time O(1).

feedback.reason: Streaming separates output volume from internal materialization.
feedback.details: The logical output remains size k even when peak internal storage is constant.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.aux_k: The sink can consume them incrementally.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.time_constant: There are still k emission actions.

items.prompt: Does proving an Ω(k) output lower bound prove that every O(k²) algorithm is optimal?

options.text: No. The lower bound only proves at least linear-in-k work; an O(k²) algorithm may contain avoidable overhead.
Yes. Any algorithm above the lower bound is optimal.
Yes, because output-sensitive problems ignore computation.
No lower bounds can ever support optimality claims.

feedback.reason: An algorithm is output-optimal only when its upper bound matches the required-output lower bound, possibly plus unavoidable input work.
feedback.details: A large gap between Ω(k) and O(k²) remains unexplained.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.yes_any: Optimality requires matching the lower bound asymptotically.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.yes_output: Search and processing overhead still matter.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.no_lower: A matching upper and lower bound can establish optimality.

items.prompt: An algorithm scans all n inputs and returns no matches, so k = 0. Can its time still be O(n)?

options.text: Yes. Output size can be zero while input examination remains necessary.
No. Zero output forces O(1) time.
No. Zero output implies O(n²).
Time always equals k.

feedback.reason: Output lower bounds are only one component of total work.
feedback.details: A tight bound may be Θ(n + k), which becomes Θ(n) when k = 0.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.no_zero: The algorithm may need to prove no match exists.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.output_equals_time: Output-sensitive bounds often include both input and output terms.

items.prompt: Which statements about output-sensitive lower bounds are correct? Select all that apply.

options.text: Materializing k constant-size results requires Ω(k) time.
Retaining k constant-size results requires Ω(k) output space.
A tight algorithm may need an additional input-processing term such as O(n + k).
Output size can always be ignored when stating time.

feedback.reason: Output-sensitive analysis combines unavoidable emission with any necessary discovery cost.
feedback.details: Ignoring k can understate both time and space.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.output_free: That can produce an impossible advertised bound.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.input_term: Finding or proving results can require scanning input.

items.prompt: Which tasks can have inherently superlinear materialized output? Select all that apply.

options.text: Return all unordered pairs of n items.
Return every subset of n items.
Return every permutation of n items.
Return one boolean saying whether a duplicate exists.

feedback.reason: Enumeration contracts can force polynomial, exponential, or factorial output.
feedback.details: Decision contracts may avoid materializing those witnesses.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.all_subsets: There are 2^n subsets and Θ(n·2^n) total elements.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.all_permutations: There are n! outputs of length n.

items.prompt: Which storage counts as output rather than auxiliary space? Select all that apply.

options.text: A list explicitly required and returned by the function.
A required result file containing all records.
A deduplication set discarded before return.
Active call frames.

feedback.reason: Role, not allocation time, determines output classification.
feedback.details: Temporary computation state remains auxiliary even if it contains result-like values.

items.prompt: Which advertised bounds are incomplete or impossible under the stated contract? Select all that apply.

options.text: Return all Θ(n²) pairs in O(n) time.
Materialize every subset in O(2^n) time while each subset is stored as its elements.
Report k matches after a full scan in O(n + k) time.
Store every permutation while claiming O(1) output space.

feedback.reason: A claimed upper bound must be at least large enough to represent the required result.
feedback.details: The O(n + k) reporting bound explicitly includes both components.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.report_matches_n_k: This is compatible with input and output work.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.all_pairs_linear: The algorithm cannot write the output that quickly.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.all_subsets_two_n: The total emitted element count is Θ(n·2^n).

items.prompt: Arrange the process for deriving an output lower bound.

elements.text: Define one output record and its representation cost.
Bound the maximum or actual number k of required records.
Conclude Ω(k) writes and, when retained, Ω(k) output space.
Compare the candidate upper bound with input and output lower bounds.

feedback.reason: Output-sensitive reasoning starts from the required representation.
feedback.details: Only then can algorithmic overhead be evaluated.

items.prompt: Arrange the verification of a claimed O(n + k) reporting algorithm.

elements.text: Verify input discovery work is O(n).
Verify each of k results is emitted with O(1) additional work.
Check that no result or input region is repeatedly rescanned.
Compare the upper bound with Ω(n) discovery and Ω(k) output obligations.

feedback.reason: The expression is tight only when hidden work does not multiply n or k.
feedback.details: Matching lower bounds can establish asymptotic optimality.

items.prompt: A reporting algorithm scans n records once and appends each of k matches with O(1) work. Select time, auxiliary space excluding output, and output space.
constraints: No other growing structure is used.

feedback.reason: The scan and output writes are separate additive work.
feedback.details: The returned list is output, while the internal state stays constant.

items.prompt: A function materializes every unordered pair of n elements. Select pair count, time lower bound, and output-space lower bound.
constraints: Each pair is stored as one constant-size record.

feedback.reason: There are n(n - 1)/2 pairs.
feedback.details: Materializing each pair forces quadratic time and output storage.

items.prompt: A function materializes all n! permutations, each of length n. Select output count, time lower bound, and output-space lower bound.

feedback.reason: There are n! outputs and each contains n elements.
feedback.details: Both writing and retaining the materialized results require n·n! element occurrences.

items.prompt: Two sorted lists of lengths n and m are scanned to report all k common-value occurrences with one linear merge. Select time, auxiliary space excluding output, and output space.
constraints: Each input pointer moves only forward and each reported occurrence is appended once.

feedback.reason: The merge discovers candidates in linear input work and emits k results.
feedback.details: This is a canonical output-sensitive bound.

items.prompt: A function enumerates every subset of n elements and stores each subset as its elements. Select subset count and total materialized output volume.
constraints: Each indexed element appears in exactly half of all subsets.

feedback.reason: Counting subsets alone understates materialization cost.
feedback.details: Total stored element occurrences are n·2^(n - 1).
